one_line_claim: The paper argues that scarce verifiable math labels should be spent first on sparse RL for a capable teacher, then converted into dense token-level supervision for a smaller deployment student through an FKL-to-OPD bridge, with student GRPO only after that bridge.
what_they_tested: The authors evaluate Qwen3-1.7B as the fixed deployment student on MATH-500, AIME 2024, and AIME 2025 using avg@16. They compare direct student GRPO, raw/SFT/RL Qwen3-8B and 14B teachers transferred through a two-stage bridge, same-size RL-teacher transfer, OPD-only and teacher-sample-SFT transfer controls, half-split post-bridge student GRPO versus replay, and a narrower Llama-3.1-8B / Llama-3.3-70B ordering check.
strongest_point: The strongest part is the controlled allocation framing: raw teachers underperform direct GRPO, SFT teachers are intermediate, RL-shaped teachers win, the two-stage bridge beats OPD-only and teacher-sample SFT on MATH, and the replay control shows post-bridge gains are not just extra updates on old data.
weakest_point: The evidence is narrow and resource-confounded: it is mostly one Qwen3 deployment size on verifiable math, with a thin Llama replication, no independently retrained-run variance, modest fixed-data margins, and no compute-matched comparison against spending the teacher-RL-plus-distillation budget on stronger student-side training.
missing_baselines: A compute-matched direct-student pipeline that spends the same wall-clock/GPU/rollout budget as teacher RL plus FKL-to-OPD, not only the same labeled examples.
A strong student SFT-warmup or verifier-filtered-SFT-plus-GRPO baseline using the same DAPO data, since practical post-training is rarely cold GRPO alone.
Reference-guided fine-tuning, self-distilled RLVR, or other recent dense-reward/data-filtering recipes run on the same Qwen3-1.7B student.
A long-context or trace-level teacher-sample distillation baseline with careful filtering and multiple samples per problem, beyond the single teacher-sample SFT route reported.
Black-box or cross-tokenizer distillation baselines for settings where teacher and student do not share a vocabulary; the proposed OPD bridge is tokenizer-family constrained.
A compute/cost table comparing direct student GRPO, full teacher-side bridge, half-split bridge plus student GRPO, and replay in H200-hours, teacher queries, and generated tokens.
missing_ablations: Multiple random data splits, training seeds, and independently retrained checkpoints; the reported error bars are over evaluation problems, not training-run variance.
Student-size and teacher-size scaling beyond Qwen3-1.7B with 8B/14B teachers and the single Llama 8B/70B check, especially stronger deployment students where cold sparse RL may be less weak.
Task-family ablations on code, tool use, instruction following, or open-ended tasks with verifiers, because the whole claim is currently anchored in math rewards.
Stage-budget sweeps for FKL length, OPD length, teacher-RL steps, Stage 3 GRPO steps, and the order or mixture of sparse and dense objectives.
Reward/verifier robustness checks, including noisy graders, different math evaluators, and train/eval deduplication or similarity analysis against DAPO-Math.
Decoding-metric sensitivity: pass@1, majority@16, best-of-N, temperature sweeps, and reasoning-length changes, not only avg@16 at one decoding setup.
Teacher-quality continuum ablations showing how transfer performance tracks teacher RL checkpoint quality and whether overtrained or miscalibrated teachers hurt the dense bridge.
red_flags: The paper calls this a reward-density principle, but the causal mechanism is inferred from endpoint comparisons; there is little direct measurement of exploration rate, reward sparsity, credit assignment quality, or student-state support mismatch.

Same-label allocation is not the same as same-resource allocation: teacher-first uses a larger teacher, teacher querying, cached logits, and extra transfer stages, so the practical prescription may be driven partly by compute availability.

Several key differences are modest relative to uncertainty: the central fixed-data teacher-side route beats half-split post-bridge student GRPO by only 0.8 MATH points, and AIME points are stated to be within standard error.
The Llama evidence is only a three-row ordering check; the paper explicitly leaves half-split, replay, OPD-only, teacher-sample SFT, and SFT-teacher Llama controls for future work.
The same-size RL-teacher result is rhetorically useful but weak: 76.5% versus 75.9% MATH and 20.6 versus 19.8 AIME 2024 are small gains, with AIME 2025 matching direct GRPO.
Reported standard errors are across evaluation problems, not across independently trained runs, so stability of the full multi-stage training recipe is unknown.
The bridge is best on MATH, but AIME after Stage 3 is closer and partly mixed, including an OPD-only AIME 2025 row that slightly exceeds the two-stage bridge for the 14B teacher.
The result may not transfer cleanly outside labs that maintain aligned model families with shared tokenizers; the paper notes the bridge requires shared tokenizer/vocabulary for OPD.
positive_signals: The paper asks a sharp operational question: where should fixed scarce verified labels enter a post-training pipeline, rather than which algorithm wins in isolation.

Controls are meaningfully targeted: raw versus SFT versus RL teachers test teacher quality; same-size teacher transfer tests scale; OPD-only and teacher-sample SFT test the bridge; replay tests whether new labels matter.
The raw-teacher rows underperform direct GRPO, which makes the main claim less like generic distillation hype and more like a specific reward-shaped-teacher claim.
The half-split design and replay control are useful because they separate fresh held-out labels from simply continuing updates on bridge data.
The authors disclose scope limits clearly: verifiable math, relatively small deployment scales, shared-tokenizer requirement, and a deliberately narrower Llama block.
Implementation details include DAPO split construction, matched training protocol fields, bridge protocol, avg@16 evaluation definition, and key GRPO/transfer hyperparameters.
The paper reports mixed caveats rather than all-win tables: post-bridge AIME cells are close, the teacher-side fixed-data margin is small, and student-side post-bridge RL remains useful.
verdict: Worth covering. This is a clean, timely post-training allocation paper with a concrete recipe and unusually relevant controls, but it should be framed as promising evidence for verifiable math rather than a general law of post-training. The episode angle is strong: scarce labels are more valuable when a larger model turns them into dense teacher behavior, but the claim still needs compute-matched baselines, retraining variance, broader tasks, and stronger cross-family controls.
citations: data/papers/arxiv-2605.12483.md:1-22 identifies the paper, metadata, and abstract claim that scarce verifiable labels should move upstream to teacher RL and then downstream as dense supervision.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12483.txt:68-78 states the reward-density argument: sparse task reward and dense teacher log-probabilities lie on the same KL-regularized objective axis, and larger models can turn sparse reward into stronger behavior.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12483.txt:97-105 defines the scope: MATH/AIME with Qwen3 and Llama families, Qwen3-1.7B deployment student, Qwen3-8B/14B teachers, Llama-3.1-8B student and Llama-3.3-70B teacher, with OPD only within shared-tokenizer families.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12483.txt:213-217 says the experiments use verl/HybridFlow, report avg@16, and give standard errors across evaluation problems.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12483.txt:257-263 reports direct GRPO across Qwen3 scales: Qwen3-1.7B reaches 75.9% MATH, while 4B/8B/14B reach much stronger endpoints.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12483.txt:290-310 reports Table 3 transfer-only endpoints: raw teachers underperform direct GRPO, SFT teachers are intermediate, RL'd 8B/14B teachers reach 79.3% and 78.6% MATH, and two one-stage transfer controls trail the bridge.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12483.txt:327-339 describes the half-split post-bridge RL and replay test, where held-out 2H student GRPO lifts MATH while replaying 1H does not.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12483.txt:341-364 reports Tables 4 and 5: RL-teacher bridge plus held-out GRPO reaches 78.5/78.7% MATH, replay stays around 75.6-75.7%, and SFT-teacher routes are weaker.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12483.txt:368-383 states the central fixed-data allocation contrast: full upstream use reaches 79.3% MATH versus 78.5% for half-bridge plus held-out student GRPO, with AIME points within standard error.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12483.txt:394-405 reports Table 6 bridge controls after Stage 3, where the two-stage bridge is best on MATH but AIME cells are closer and partly mixed.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12483.txt:430-440 lists the authors' limitations: verifiable math only, small deployment scales, uncertain larger-scale behavior, shared-tokenizer bridge requirement, no code/open-ended-task claims, no optimal lambda schedule, and narrow Llama controls.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12483.txt:749-757 reports Table 8 Llama replication: raw 70B transfer scores 55.4% MATH versus 59.8% direct GRPO, while RL'd 70B transfer reaches 62.1%, with OPD constrained to shared tokenizer/vocabulary.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12483.txt:758-792 gives data-split, matched-training, bridge-protocol, and evaluation details, including that R5-half and R7 match data count/update count except for fresh versus replay examples and that error bars are not retraining variance.
